1: Jehoshaphat had great riches and honor, and he made a marriage alliance with Ahab. The verb wayyithattēn (made a marriage alliance) is a reflexive formation from ḥātān (father-in-law/son-in-law) — the forging of covenant kinship through intermarriage. The Chronicler introduces this alliance immediately after Jehoshaphat’s prosperity to signal the theological tension: wealth and honor do not protect against the seduction of dangerous alliances. The marriage of Jehoshaphat’s son to Ahab’s daughter (Athaliah, 2 Chronicles 21:6) will nearly destroy the Davidic line in the next generation.
2: After some years Jehoshaphat goes down to visit Ahab at Samaria — the direction word (wayyērēḏ, “went down”) signals a spiritual descent as much as a geographical one. Ahab slaughters sheep and cattle for Jehoshaphat in abundance, then wayyᵉsîtēhû (enticed/incited him) to go up against Ramoth-gilead. The verb sût (to incite/entice) is the same word used of Satan inciting David to the census (1 Chronicles 21:1) — the Chronicler signals that this alliance is spiritually dangerous.
3: Jehoshaphat says: kāmônî ḵāmôḵā ûḵᵉʿammᵉḵā ʿammî wᵉʿimmᵉḵā bamilḥāmāh — “I am as you are, my people as your people. We will be with you in the war.” This is the covenant solidarity formula — almost verbatim identical to Ruth’s words to Naomi (Ruth 1:16 in reverse: “your people shall be my people”) — here applied to a military alliance with the apostate northern kingdom. The same formula that expresses loyal covenant love is here misapplied to a dangerous political marriage.
4: Jehoshaphat adds: dᵉrāš-nāʾ kᵉyôm ʾet-dᵉḇar YHWH — “Inquire first for the word of YHWH.” The characteristic Chronicler’s verb dāraš (seek/inquire) is Jehoshaphat’s instinct even in the midst of a compromised alliance. He insists on prophetic consultation before military action — the right instinct, though it will not prevent his error. The kᵉyôm (“today/first”) emphasizes the urgency: do not proceed without YHWH’s word.
5: Ahab assembles 400 prophets who unanimously predict victory: “Go up, for God will give it into the hand of the king.” The suspicious unanimity of 400 prophets — all agreeing — already signals their inauthenticity. True prophets in the OT are rarely unanimous, especially when they are telling kings what kings want to hear. Jeremiah will later identify the false prophet precisely as the one who speaks “peace, peace, when there is no peace” (Jeremiah 6:14; 2 Chronicles 8:11).
6: Jehoshaphat asks: hăʾên pōh nāḇîʾ lYHWH ʿôḏ — “Is there not here another prophet of the LORD of whom we may inquire?” The distinction between YHWH’s prophet and Ahab’s prophets is unstated but felt: Jehoshaphat senses the difference between prophets who serve the king and the prophet who speaks YHWH’s word. The ʿôḏ (“another/still”) implies he already doubts the 400.
7: Ahab: ʿôḏ ʾîš-ʾeḥāḏ liḏrōš ʾet-YHWH mēʾittô wᵉʾănî śᵉnēʾtîhû — “There is yet one man by whom we may inquire of YHWH, but I hate him, because he never prophesies good concerning me, but always evil.” Ahab’s self-indicting statement: he knows the difference between YHWH’s true prophet (Micaiah) and his court prophets, but his criterion for rejecting Micaiah is personal — he speaks unwelcome truth. The prophetic office of truth-telling that costs its bearer persecution reaches its apex in Jesus (John 8:40; Matthew 23:29-31).
8: Ahab calls Micaiah ben Imlah. Micaiah is named and his father named — he is a real, locatable person, not a generic prophet. The Chronicler preserves historical specificity even in a narrative borrowed from Kings. The king’s officer who is sent for Micaiah is told to bring him — Micaiah is apparently under some constraint, perhaps under a previous prophetic arrest (1 Kings 22:26-27 gives the background).
9: The two kings sit on their thrones, arrayed in their robes, at the threshing floor at the entrance to the gate of Samaria. The threshing floor (gōren) as an official meeting place recalls the threshing floor of Ornan where David’s altar was built (1 Chronicles 21:18) and where Solomon’s temple would stand — a location with atonement overtones. The 400 prophets prophesying before the kings is a spectacle of royal religion in all its compromised splendor.
10: Zedekiah son of Chenaanah makes himself iron horns and declares: kōh-ʾāmar YHWH bᵉʾēlleh tᵉnaggaḥ ʾet-ʾĂrām — “Thus says the LORD: With these you shall push the Syrians until they are destroyed.” The symbolic action of iron horns is a prophetic theater-act, but it invokes the LORD’s authority without having received the LORD’s word. The false prophet’s greatest danger is not outright fabrication but the confident use of divine language for self-serving predictions.
11: All 400 prophets prophesy the same: ʿălēh Rāmōt Gilʿāḏ wᵉhaṣlîaḥ — “Go up to Ramoth-gilead and succeed.” The verbal root ṣlḥ (to succeed/prosper) is the Chronicler’s key word for the outcome of covenant faithfulness. That 400 prophets use it here for a venture that will end in disaster is the Chronicler’s irony: ṣlḥ without YHWH’s actual word is empty.
12: The officer tells Micaiah: the 400 prophets speak with one voice — “let your word be like one of theirs” — a blatant invitation to conform. Ûḏabbēr ṭôḇ (“speak good”) is the pressure that every true prophet faces: accommodate the consensus. Micaiah’s response (v13) sets up the great contrast between prophetic conformity and prophetic faithfulness.
13: Ḥay-YHWH kî ʾēt ʾăšer-yōʾmar ʾĕlōhay ʾōtô ʾᵃḏabbēr — “As YHWH lives, whatever my God says, that I will speak.” The oath formula ḥay-YHWH invokes the living God as witness to the prophet’s integrity. The switch from “YHWH” to ʾĕlōhay (“my God”) in the middle of the verse is significant: Micaiah speaks in terms of personal relationship — this is his God, not merely the God of the institution.
14: Micaiah arrives and immediately mirrors the 400: ʿălû wᵉhaṣlîḥû wᵉyinnāṯᵉnû bᵉyaḏᵉḵem — “Go up and succeed; they will be given into your hand.” This is deliberate irony — Micaiah uses the exact words of the false prophets, and Ahab immediately recognizes it as mockery (v15). The true prophet deliberately adopts the form of false prophecy to expose its emptiness, forcing Ahab to request the actual truth.
15: Ahab adjures Micaiah: kammeh pᵉʿāmîm ʾănî mašbîʿᵉḵā ʾăšer lōʾ tᵉḏabbēr ʾēlay raq-ʾemet — “How many times shall I make you swear that you speak to me nothing but the truth in the name of the LORD?” Ahab knows Micaiah is mocking him. The king who has rejected YHWH’s word still recognizes it when he hears its absence. The conscience that recognizes truth even when the will refuses it is one of Scripture’s most poignant ironies.
16: Micaiah’s true vision: rāʾîtî ʾet-kol-Yiśrāʾēl nᵉpûṣîm ʿal-hehārîm kᵉṣōʾn ʾăšer ʾên-lāhem rōʿeh — “I saw all Israel scattered on the mountains as sheep that have no shepherd.” The image of leaderless sheep (ṣōʾn ʾăšer ʾên-lāhem rōʿeh) is the OT’s most frequent pastoral image for a people in crisis (Numbers 27:17; Ezekiel 34:5). It is the direct background for Jesus’ compassion on the crowds who were “like sheep without a shepherd” (Matthew 9:36; Mark 6:34).
17: Ahab says to Jehoshaphat: “Did I not tell you that he would not prophesy good concerning me, but evil?” — hālōʾ ʾāmartî ʾēleḵā lōʾ yitnabbēʾ ʿālay ṭôḇ kî ʾim-rāʿ. Ahab demonstrates the perverse self-knowledge of the willfully disobedient: he knows the true prophet speaks unwelcome truth, he has asked for the truth, and now that he has heard it he retreats to resentment. The knowledge that does not lead to obedience only deepens condemnation (John 9:41; Romans 1:21).
18: Micaiah continues: “Therefore hear the word of the LORD: I saw the LORD sitting on his throne, and all the host of heaven standing on his right and on his left.” The heavenly throne-room vision grants the prophet a view behind the visible world — the ṣᵉḇāʾ haššāmayim (host of heaven) arrayed before YHWH parallels Isaiah’s throne-room vision (Isaiah 6:1-3) and the divine council imagery of Job 1-2. The prophet who has access to this scene speaks with a different authority than those who only mirror the king’s wishes.
19: YHWH asks: mî yᵉfatteh ʾet-ʾAḥāḇ — “Who will entice Ahab that he may go up and fall at Ramoth-gilead?” The divine permission of deception — YHWH soliciting the means of Ahab’s ruin — is theologically significant. The pittāh (to entice/seduce) is the same verb used of Eve’s temptation (Genesis 3:13), Potiphar’s wife (Genesis 39:7), and Samson’s seduction. God does not cause the deception but permits and employs the spiritual reality that judgment on the persistently wicked sometimes involves confirmation of their chosen blindness (Isaiah 6:10; Romans 1:24-28).
20: Various members of the heavenly council propose options; then a rûaḥ (spirit) comes forward: wayyēṣēʾ hārûaḥ wayyaʿămoḏ lipnê YHWH — “Then a spirit came forward and stood before the LORD.” The personified spirit before the divine council echoes the śāṭān figure in Job 1:6 and Zechariah 3:1-2 — a spiritual being operating within the divine court with a specific function. The heavenly council scene asserts divine sovereignty: nothing happens outside YHWH’s knowledge and permission, even the deception of apostate kings.
21: The spirit says: ʾēṣēʾ wᵉhāyîtî rûaḥ šeqer bᵉpî kol-nᵉḇîʾāyw — “I will go out and be a lying spirit in the mouth of all his prophets.” Rûaḥ šeqer (lying/false spirit) — YHWH says, “You are to entice him, and you shall succeed.” The divine permission here is not divine authorship of the lie: the false prophets’ willingness to deceive is already established; YHWH permits the spirit to exploit that established disposition. The theological principle: God’s hardening judgments confirm and deepen what the human will has already chosen (Exodus 4:21; Romans 9:18).
22: YHWH nātan rûaḥ šeqer bᵉpî nᵉḇîʾêḵā ʾēlleh wYHWH dibbēr ʿāleḵā rāʿāh — “The LORD has put a lying spirit in the mouth of these your prophets. The LORD has declared disaster for you.” Micaiah’s conclusion integrates divine sovereignty and human responsibility: the false prophets are genuinely lying (their guilt is real), yet YHWH’s purposes encompass their lie. The NT holds the same tension: Judas betrayed Christ by his own will, yet it happened “according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God” (Acts 2:23).
23: Zedekiah son of Chenaanah strikes Micaiah on the cheek and demands: ʾê-zeh dereḵ ʿāḇar rûaḥ-YHWH mēʾittî lᵉḏabbēr ʾōtāḵ — “Which way did the Spirit of YHWH go from me to speak to you?” The physical violence against the true prophet who contradicts the court prophets anticipates the persecution of Jesus (Matthew 26:67-68; John 18:22-23): the blow to the face is the false prophet’s response to prophetic exposure. Micaiah’s reply — “You will see on that day when you go from chamber to chamber to hide yourself” — sets the self-validating test of the true prophecy.
24: wayyōʾmer Mîḵāyᵉhû hinnᵉḵā rōʾeh bayyôm hahûʾ ʾăšer tābōʾ ḥeder bᵉḥeḏer lᵉhēḥābēʾ — “Behold, you shall see on that day when you go from chamber to chamber to hide yourself.” The self-fulfillment criterion of true prophecy (Deuteronomy 18:21-22): Micaiah offers the proof of his authority — when the battle ends in disaster, Zedekiah will be the one hiding. The contrast with the false prophet is not just content but consequence: false prophecy creates no accountability; true prophecy stakes its authority on historical outcomes.
25: Ahab orders Micaiah put in prison and fed minimum rations: lāḥem laḥaṣ ûmayim laḥaṣ ʿaḏ-šûḇî bᵉšālôm — “bread of affliction and water of affliction, until I return in peace.” The imprisonment of the prophet echoes Jeremiah (Jeremiah 37:15), John the Baptist (Matthew 14:3), and ultimately Christ (John 18:12). The pattern of power imprisoning truth recurs because every institutional authority that has committed to a course of action finds the prophetic voice inconvenient.
26: Micaiah’s final word: ʾim-šôḇ tāšûḇ bᵉšālôm lōʾ-dibbēr YHWH bî — “If you return in peace, the LORD has not spoken by me.” This is the prophet’s signature: I stake my prophetic authority on this word. If Ahab returns safely, I am a false prophet. The courage of this declaration — made to a king who has just ordered imprisonment — is the hallmark of the true prophetic vocation: not self-preservation but YHWH’s honor.
27: wayyōʾmer šimʿû ʿammîm kullām — “And he said, ‘Hear, all you peoples!’” Micaiah’s public declaration that the assembled nations will witness the outcome broadens the scope of the prophetic test from king and court to all peoples. The true prophetic word is not private — it is addressed to the widest possible audience as a public claim about YHWH’s governance of history.
28: The kings of Israel and Judah go up to Ramoth-gilead. The battle proceeds despite Micaiah’s warning — the momentum of human ambition and the pressure of 400 court prophets overwhelm one voice of truth. The narrative logic of prophetic rejection reaching its inevitable conclusion is the Chronicler’s demonstration that rejection of YHWH’s word does not change its content; it only changes the hearer’s relationship to its outcome.
29: Ahab says to Jehoshaphat: hitḥappēś wābōʾ bammiḥlāmāh wᵉʾattāh lᵉḇôš bᵉḡāḏêḵā — “I will disguise myself and go into battle, but you wear your robes.” Ahab’s strategy to evade Micaiah’s prophecy by disguise is the attempt to outmaneuver divine foreknowledge — as if YHWH’s word is about Ahab-in-his-robes rather than about Ahab himself. The irony is complete: Ahab’s disguise does not save him; a random arrow finds him anyway (v33). Providence cannot be evaded by costume.
30: The king of Syria commands: attack no one small or great, only the king of Israel. The strategic targeting of the king — his single person amid the whole army — is the human equivalent of YHWH’s specific judgment on Ahab. The Syrian army obeys orders by targeting the king; the random arrow disobeys orders by hitting a disguised man who happens to be the king. Both converge on the same divinely ordained outcome.
31: The Syrian chariot commanders pursue Jehoshaphat thinking he is the king of Israel — Jehoshaphat cries out (wayyizʿaq), and YHWH helps him (waYHWH ʿăzārô wᵉsîtēm ʾĕlōhîm mēʾālāyw). The same verb sût (entice/turn away) used of Ahab’s enticement to battle (v2) is used here of God turning the Syrians from Jehoshaphat — a verbal echo: Jehoshaphat was enticed by Ahab; God removes the Syrian entrapment from Jehoshaphat. The cry to YHWH in extremity is the one act that distinguishes Jehoshaphat from Ahab.
32: The Syrian commanders see that Jehoshaphat is not the king of Israel and turn back from pursuing him. The divine rescue follows the cry for help — prayer at the moment of extremity produces divine intervention. The Chronicler presents this as a direct causal connection: wayyizʿaq (he cried out) → waYHWH ʿăzārô (YHWH helped him). The cry of distress to YHWH is never wasted (Psalms 34:17; 2 Chronicles 50:15; 2 Chronicles 107:6).
33: wᵉʾîš māšaḵ baqqešet lᵉtummô wayyakkeh ʾet-melek Yiśrāʾēl — “But a certain man drew his bow at random and struck the king of Israel.” Lᵉtummô means “in his simplicity/at random” — without intention, without aim, without knowledge of his target. The “random” arrow that finds the one man in disguise among thousands is the Chronicler’s most precise statement of providential specificity: what humans call chance is YHWH’s precision. There are no random events in the narrative of divine judgment (Proverbs 16:33).
34: Ahab is propped up in his chariot facing the Syrians until evening — wayyimot bāʿereḇ — and he dies at sunset. The image of the mortally wounded king sustained in his chariot, still facing the battle, until sunset — when the fighting ends (Deuteronomy 24:15 establishes sunset as the end of the day’s work) — is one of the most powerful death scenes in Chronicles. Ahab’s death fulfills Micaiah’s word precisely (v16): Israel scattered, the king dead.
